response:
Here are the main issues with using  
```js
function deepClone(obj) {
  return JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj));
}
```  
and some concrete suggestions for fixing or improving it:

1. Data‐type limitations  
   • Functions, Symbols and undefined will be lost.  
   • Date becomes a string, RegExp becomes an empty object, Map/Set become plain objects/arrays.  
   • NaN, Infinity and –0 lose their special semantics (NaN becomes null, Infinity becomes null, –0 becomes 0).  
   • Typed arrays, ArrayBuffers, Blobs, Errors, class instances, etc., all get mangled or dropped.  
   • Prototype chain is not preserved.

   How to fix  
   – Use the built-in structuredClone (modern browsers and Node 17+):  
     ```js
     const clone = structuredClone(obj);
     ```  
     It handles Dates, RegExps, Maps, Sets, ArrayBuffers, circular refs, etc.  
   – Or use a battle-tested library (e.g. Lodash’s cloneDeep) if you need wide compatibility:  
     ```js
     import cloneDeep from 'lodash/cloneDeep';
     const clone = cloneDeep(obj);
     ```  
   – If you have special needs (e.g. class instances), write a custom recursive cloner that copies prototypes and calls constructors.

2. Circular references  
   • JSON.stringify will throw `TypeError: Converting circular structure to JSON` if `obj` contains cycles.  

   How to fix  
   – Use structuredClone or a library that tracks references.  
   – Roll your own with a `WeakMap` to track seen objects:
     ```js
     function deepClone(obj, seen = new WeakMap()) {
       if (obj === null || typeof obj !== 'object') return obj;
       if (seen.has(obj)) return seen.get(obj);
       const copy = Array.isArray(obj) ? [] : Object.create(Object.getPrototypeOf(obj));
       seen.set(obj, copy);
       for (const key of Reflect.ownKeys(obj)) {
         copy[key] = deepClone(obj[key], seen);
       }
       return copy;
     }
     ```

3. Performance and Denial‐of‐Service risk  
   • Serializing then parsing large or deeply nested objects can be very expensive.  
   • If you expose this to untrusted input (e.g. web API), a malicious payload could trigger huge memory or CPU usage.  

   How to fix  
   – Set a maximum depth or size guard before cloning.  
   – Use incremental algorithms or streaming if you must handle very large structures.  
   – Prefer structuredClone (it’s optimized in engines).

4. Loss of property descriptors and non‐enumerable properties  
   • JSON.stringify only copies enumerable string‐keyed properties. get/set accessors, non-enumerable props, and symbol‐keyed props are dropped.  

   How to fix  
   – Use `Reflect.ownKeys` + `Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor` + `Object.defineProperty` in a custom cloner.  
   – Or rely on libraries that preserve descriptors if you need them.

5. Prototype pollution (less likely but possible in certain patterns)  
   • If you merge the result back into existing objects with `Object.assign` or similar, a payload containing `__proto__` could modify your application’s prototypes.  

   How to fix  
   – Always validate or sanitize keys before merging user-controlled data.  
   – Avoid assigning untrusted clones directly onto existing objects’ prototypes.

Summary of improvements  
• Whenever you need a “real” deep clone that handles all built-in types and circular refs, prefer structuredClone or a well-tested library.  
• If you roll your own, make sure to:  
  – Handle all data types you care about.  
  – Preserve prototypes and descriptors if needed.  
  – Track already‐visited objects to break cycles.  
  – Optionally enforce size/depth limits for untrusted data.
